title: About APTlantis
summary: APTlantis is a unified open-source archive, mirror network, and automation platform that preserves software ecosystems, datasets, and cultural knowledge while empowering developers with fast, reliable access to information.
mission: APTlantis exists to preserve open knowledge and make it permanently accessible. Through a fusion of mirrors, datasets, automation pipelines, and AI-driven metadata systems, APTlantis creates a stable, verifiable, and future-proof library of modern software ecosystems and digital culture. The project is built on transparency, open tooling, and the belief that long-term access to information should be simple, fast, and freely available.

content_sections.heading: What APTlantis Is
content_sections.body: APTlantis is a hybrid platform combining public mirrors, curated datasets, and intelligent automation. It hosts operating system repositories, language package archives, specialized datasets, torrent rescues, historical collections, and live dashboards powered by real metadata. APTlantis emphasizes redundancy, validation, and open data formats to ensure that knowledge remains available long after original sources disappear.

content_sections.heading: A Unified Storage & Mirror Network
content_sections.body: APTlantis syncs and hosts more than 55 Linux distributions and dozens of programming-language ecosystems, including CPAN, Hackage, PyPI top packages, Flathub refs, and specialized archives like TempleOS collections and Rust crate indexes. All repositories are mirrored through high-performance servers and made accessible via HTTP, rsync, and internal automation workers.

content_sections.heading: Built for Longevity
content_sections.body: Everything is designed with preservation in mind. Files are validated using multi-hash verification, manifests are auto-generated, and metadata is normalized into JSON-LD, TOML, CSV, and other open formats. If an upstream source vanishes, APTlantis keeps the knowledge alive.

content_sections.heading: Automation & AI-Assisted Infrastructure
content_sections.body: APTlantis is powered by a custom ecosystem of automation agents and 'LLM Secretaries' that watch directories, analyze datasets, generate metadata, summarize project states, and orchestrate pipelines. The system includes fine-tuning workflows, dataset scrapers, torrent reconstruction tools, and scanners that classify, validate, or rebuild archives.

content_sections.heading: Developer-Focused Philosophy
content_sections.body: APTlantis is built with the mindset of a hobbyist developer who values practicality, transparency, and open-source ethics. The entire platform is designed so that a single individual can effectively maintain a massive, complex archival system using smart tooling, AI helpers, and automation rather than a large team.

content_sections.heading: Why It Matters
content_sections.body: Software ecosystems move fast—sometimes too fast for their own good. When a distribution dies, an API shuts down, or a repository is abandoned, entire generations of code can evaporate. APTlantis prevents that loss. It ensures that researchers, developers, archivists, and future explorers can still access the building blocks of open knowledge decades from now.

faq.question: What does APTlantis actually host?
faq.answer: APTlantis maintains a collection of operating-system mirrors, package repositories, programming-language ecosystems, and specialized datasets. It also stores metadata, manifest files, torrent reconstructions, and archival snapshots of software ecosystems.

faq.question: Is everything open source?
faq.answer: Yes. APTlantis focuses exclusively on open-source software, freely distributable datasets, public domain works, and material that is legally shareable under permissive licensing. Anything proprietary or unclear is excluded by default.

faq.question: Why does APTlantis exist?
faq.answer: To preserve ecosystems, prevent bit-rot, and give developers fast access to reliable, long-term mirrors. Many projects vanish over time; APTlantis makes sure their knowledge doesn't.

faq.question: How is APTlantis maintained?
faq.answer: The system is built to be maintainable by a single operator through automation pipelines, AI agents, file watchers, and standardized schemas. Hardware includes a home server fleet, remote dedibox servers, and cloud-tunneled endpoints handled through Caddy and Cloudflare.

faq.question: Can I access the mirrors openly?
faq.answer: Yes. All public mirrors are accessible via HTTP and rsync, and the platform exposes human-friendly browsing interfaces and machine-readable metadata schemas.

faq.question: Does APTlantis track users or collect data?
faq.answer: No. APTlantis does not track usage, log personal data, or require authentication for public mirrors. Privacy is a core design requirement.
